topics.title: New Sitcom and Media Controversy
topics.summary: The show discusses the development of a new radio sitcom with Sam Simon, upcoming guest appearances on the channel, and various letters regarding media censorship and personal controversies.

topics.title: Satellite Radio and Production Debates
topics.summary: The studio discusses the growth of satellite radio, the development of a new sitcom, and an internal dispute regarding a staff member's past claims about a pay-per-view stunt.

topics.title: Homocop Outtakes and Radio Production
topics.summary: The studio reviews outtakes from the production of "Homocop" while discussing upcoming radio projects, listener feedback on satellite radio, and personal anecdotes regarding high school experiences.

topics.title: Rolling Stone Cover Comparison
topics.summary: The studio discusses a past Rolling Stone photoshoot featuring Howard Stern and a thorny crown that was not published due to concerns over religious backlash, contrasting it with a similar recent cover featuring Kanye West.

topics.title: Rolling Stone Cover Controversy
topics.summary: The studio discusses the historical rejection of a Howard Stern photo in a crown of thorns and compares it to the contemporary acceptance of an identical image featuring Kanye West.

topics.title: Radio Station Rivalries and Host Interactions
topics.summary: The studio explores tensions with neighboring radio stations, discusses the security surrounding Howard Stern's studio, and shares anecdotes about personal habits and listener interactions.

topics.title: Radio Censorship and Content Management
topics.summary: The studio discusses the evolution of on-air censorship, comparing the use of a fan as a content monitor versus a lawyer after stricter regulations followed the Janet Jackson incident.

topics.title: Dead-Air Dave and Studio Transitions
topics.summary: The studio features a discussion regarding the transition of a former producer, the technical challenges of a new studio setup, and various ideas for future radio segments.

topics.title: Promotional Items and Studio Logistics
topics.summary: The studio discusses the arrival of various promotional items, including alcohol from Bacardi, a collection of books, and adult films, while addressing technical issues with equipment and internal staff dynamics.

topics.title: Sexual Experiences and Radio Business
topics.summary: The studio features a discussion regarding personal sexual preferences, the logistics of providing business cards for show submissions, and a comedic skit involving a caller reacting to a rival radio station.

topics.title: Radio Show Production and Guest Announcements
topics.summary: The studio discusses upcoming segments, a new radio sitcom being developed with Sam Simon, the arrival of guest Danny Bonaducci, and various promotional items including deodorant samples.

topics.title: Radio Show Segment on Celebrity News and Local
topics.summary: The studio discusses upcoming news segments including a profile of Mark Spriggan, the latest Rolling Stone cover featuring Kanye West, and an upcoming animal adoption event at Sapa Restaurant.

topics.title: Digital Camera Gifts and Show Feedback
topics.summary: The studio discusses the receipt of a new digital camera from Dr. Collabro, listener feedback regarding Bob Levy's presence on the show, and various segments of the program.

topics.title: Media Commentary and Celebrity News
topics.summary: The studio discusses listener feedback regarding a guest segment, the impact of American Idol on television ratings, and various news stories involving celebrities and reality television contestants.

topics.title: Media News and Current Events
topics.summary: The studio covers various topics including the Disney-Pixar merger, reports on celebrity deaths, legal debates over surveillance and judicial appointments, climate change data, and upcoming entertainment specials.

transcript.text: Yeah, good morning everybody. Well, here we are. It's Wednesday and it's the Howard Stern Show, top of the hour.
Yeah.
Good morning everybody. I don't even know where to begin. I've been sitting here for the last 25 minutes just listening to tape.
My theory that we would go on satellite and disappear forever has gotten me nowhere.
More on the news than I've ever been.
You're not much of a scientist. Your theories don't work out very well.
No, they don't.
But I'll tell you what. I feel loose as a goof and I'm ready to entertain.
How did Homocop come out, Freddy, after our little session yesterday?
That was fun, that was like...
The good news, it was fun to record. The bad news is it's now in the hands of Scott and the editing is very tedious from what I understand.
I thought that these guys with their digital can lay stuff in...
Apparently Pro Tools is giving Scott a little bit of a headache.
Is Scott not a Pro Tools guy?
He is, but I guess not as good as he could be.
Well, give it to someone else then. Give it to Jerry, you know, Pro Tools, like it's the back of his hand.
Yeah, that's a question for Scott. I thought he was on that and when I went in this morning, he says, "Don't even ask."
Don't ask. That's a half hour job.
I don't know because I'm not in the Pro Tools thing, so...
Give it over to Jerry and give Scott something simpler.
You might have to.
Well, how's he gonna get good if he never works with it?
He will. I need something quick. I need Jerry to do it.
Because Scott's not familiar with Pro Tools.
Well, when did he get it? I mean, how long has he had to work on it?
The last seven years.
We had it done yesterday morning. All the voice tracks.
Yeah.
So the assembly should take about a half hour on Pro Tools.
Oh.
If he's having trouble...
According to that though, he said it's multi-track, whatever that means.
Oh, it is. It is M.J. Fred?
Yeah, it's multi-track.
It's multi-track.
For some reason, that makes the task harder.
It's not harder. Just let Jerry do the complicated one until Scott gets up to speed on Pro Tools.
He doesn't want to come in here and tell you about the problem.
If I was Scott...
He's probably smoking right now.
And then if I was Scott, instead of being on the Stern Fan Network every five minutes,
what I would do is just practice Pro Tools until I was a master at it.
Is he on the Stern Fan Network?
He's on a lot of these websites. I used to run into him on a bulletin board all the time.
He's very busy on the Internet.
Who in the hell can deal with being on that site?
He's on the Internet.
I thought for you, you were saying like originally, it was like the after show.
There's a lot of people... You can't find... At 11 o'clock, you can't find anybody.
It's like a ghost town.
Yeah, he was busy with the after show yesterday. I dragged his ass out of there.
Because he was in there lying.
Lying?
Yeah, he's a mess.
He was bullshitting.
Yeah, he was. Did you hear it?
I was standing right next to you, yeah. He was bullshitting.
Scott the Engineer was on there saying how he never was going to take it in the ass from that porn star.
Bullshit.
Yeah, I mean bullshit.
Hey, I knew he'd change the story.
When John said that he was going to have Scott on to talk about it, I said he'll revise history.
Now, Scott the Engineer, $250,000, was going to take it in the ass from Lexington Steel on pay-per-view.
It was done, yes.
It was a done deal. And now he's saying he was never going to do it.
He did it to get under Tom Chiusano's skin.
Sure.
He's lying.
He did it for money.
Now it's a bit.
Well, I had a great day yesterday for the channel for Howard 100, Howard 101.
I had a great discussion with the, of course, great Sam Simon, who's the guy who developed and wrote and created The Simpsons and also the Drew Carey show.
As well as he was involved in the creation of Cheers and the Tracy Ellman show as well.
Now, what does that mean when he says he's involved in the creation?
There's a bunch of people.
There's a bunch of people.
It's like 10 guys, you know.
So anyway, I'm talking to Sam and Sam is now agreed to write our first radio sitcom that'll be debuting here as soon as he's ready with it.
Now, what is this one based on?
It's called Really Desperate Housewives and it's based on what happens when Artie, Howard, Fred, Robin and Gary, what happens in their households after they leave for starring our significant others.
You're kidding.
Whatever significant others doesn't want to do it, well, we'll get an access.
What about the fact that we don't live together, though?
No, it doesn't matter.
You just create it.
This is Fix-It and the land of Fix-It.
Sam started to tell me about it.
It's the funniest thing.
Dana is miserable because she spends her days searching for Artie wherever he passed out the night before.
It's just really, really funny.
I think that's why.
Yeah.
Oh, it's absolutely why.
You know what?
The whole show is lively.
I had the great pleasure of writing a sitcom with Sam once.
His work ethic is like yours.
Me and Frank Sebastiano wrote a sitcom with him, a pilot.
He fucking typed it.
He sat down and typed it while me and Frank would yell out lines.
I mean, he's a great guy to be in business with.
He's not only brilliant, he's got a great work ethic.
Well, I look forward to Sam Simon, who has now going to be writing the first sitcom.
Breaking New Ground.
That's right.
Well, that is exciting.
That's very exciting.
It's really funny because Beth, after I leave, plots my murder so she can get my money, but she has to get me to marry her.
She's still got to get that piece done.
She starts poisoning my engagement chicken, but there's some great stuff in there, and Mr. X hates you.
Well, it sounds like all my spouses hate each other.
Oh, yeah.
And it's a wild story.
The other spouses are afraid that that's going to kill me because how are their spouses going to make a living?
There's a whole thing going on.
So they're trying to keep you alive, even though they hate us.
Right.
Well, that's cool.
And we were even talking about George Takei being the neighbor.
He's over the fence.
He's over the fence.
What?
We all live together?
And Allison Norris, who, of course, has some acting experience, should be good for this.
Oh, you should jump at this.
And when Fred comes home, he never talks.
He just plays sound effects.
It's really -- Sam started to tell me his story, and I said --
He came up with it.
Yeah, yeah.
I'm so excited about seeing really, really Desperate Housewives.
He has a name for it, too.
Uh-huh.
You know, that's the -- we're going to be having many great --
That sounds funny to me.
I can't wait to hear what he's putting down.
And you will have your first radio sitcom.
Now, do we ever appear in the film?
We will appear occasionally, but it's not about us.
I understand that.
I just wondered if we would ever pop up.
Yes, you'll pop up from time to time, but --
You might not want to.
You might not want to.
So I just was listening to it, and I'm laughing my ass off.
And that was exciting.
Also exciting news for the channel.
You know, our favorite television show is Breaking Bonaducci.
Yes.
Danny, because he's so whacked out of his skull.
So completely whacked out of his skull.
Fire!
He was fired from his radio job, and he's going to come in here next week on our show
and then do a week of radio right here on Howard 100 and 101.
Oh, cool.
Is that like an audition?
But no, not an audition, but he's got to tape some stuff for his television show.
He's going to come in and do a week of radio and --
And that'll be a part of the series?
Yeah, it'll be part of the series, too.
So Danny will be by, and I'm looking forward --
I think he's going to be on -- don't quote me on this,
but I think from 1 o'clock to 4 o'clock on 101 -- I'm not sure.
I've got to --
Get the schedule straight.
It's all coming in, and there's a lot of new programming that'll be happening.
Here's what he's going to do.
He's going to shake our foundations.
We will not be the same.
Yeah, he's quite volatile.
Now, is he going to work alone, or is he going to have a team?
He has people, and he's put his own thing together.
So Danny Bonaducci will be doing -- if you call in and you're part of it,
you'll be part of his VH1 show, and he's going to be here for the next week,
and it should be insane.
Sounds interesting.
Probably kill himself right on the air.
Artie, there's another show for you to visit.
He actually will have five people with him,
and each day he'll kill one of them on the air until he's down to himself on Friday.
So that's the exciting part.
Interesting premise.
Want to be in radio?
Like, as if we got in the room and killed each other and see who was left standing.
I had a really encouraging, great conversation with a kid yesterday.
This kid, Joey Nelms, who I used to be in a sketch group with in the early '90s,
he lives in L.A. now, and he writes trailers for Warner Brothers.
Like, it's a pretty good gig, and he's a big fan of the show,
and I was asking him, you know, do you have series?
He goes, dude, I have three of them.
He's got the S-15, he's got the one in the car and the one in the house,
and it struck me.
He goes, I got a bunch of buddies who are all going to do the same exact thing.
They're going to get -- he goes, I had to get it.
There's no way I couldn't have it, and it struck me.
If I wasn't on the show, I would do the same exact thing.
Yeah, I would too, eh?
You know, and I'm sure there's a lot of people out there about to do it.
He says he's got about 10 friends about to do the same exact thing.
Yeah, a lot of people now, especially with the coverage we're getting,
are wondering what the hell they're missing.
And, you know, so more and more people are joining this little family as we go along.
People are weird, though, because, you know, they'll start to go through this withdrawal,
because that's what I'm hearing out there with people who didn't sign up yet.
They're like, well, what's going on?
I'm like, I'm not telling you anything.
I mean, this is a guy -- this is a guy who's got, you know, he's got a good gig.
He's not like some loser sitting at home over there.
He said he had to have the show, you know, so when he's got it, he's got three of them.
Yeah, and then when he signs up, he's going to be pleasantly surprised
how good the rest of the service is, and then guess what?
He's no longer going to be a terrestrial radio user.
He's going to be a satellite radio user.
Well, I'm calling this crack, because once you get on it, you can't get off.
Right.
Hey, as Danny Bonaducci says --
Hold on one second.
Yeah.
You listen to me, Jeff.
There are men in my wife's room wearing less than a f***ing turtle neck.
I will come there, and I will shake your foundation.
I will shake the f***ing rafters.
Nobody will be the same.
That's the energy we need on that show.
That's amazing.
I want him completely whacked out of his skull, calling his wife
and saying he's going to shake people's foundation.
Well, what you need to do then is set up like some kind of a bachelor party every day for his wife.
Okay.
I'll go to a bachelor party every day.
Yeah, Artie, you have to dance for Gretchen.
His wife is cute.
You're all very cute.
Very sexy.
I like her.
So that's going to be fun on the channel.
And as we grow, there will be more and more announcements.
It's going to take time.
We're trying to get top people to write our sitcoms, our cartoons.
Sam Simon is exciting.
Yeah.
And Sam had some great ideas.
So I'm excited that he's now part of the Howard 101 family,
and we'll see what comes out of that association.
Um, where do I begin?
My God, there's so much to talk about.
Yesterday, after I blew up on the air about Chauncey
and the report on Fox News and all this other bullshit that was going on,
after I blew up about that, there's now about 50 television reports
that I could play you based on that.
But I'm going to skip it only because it gets boring and old.
We've covered it, and that's the end of it.
The only thing that I will say is I opened up the New York Post today,
and, um, there are letters to the editor all about me.
It's only about me.
And it says, "Schlock, Jock, Stern. Too sassy for Sirius?"
Whatever that means.
Let me read you some of the letters that are in the New York Post today.
This is from a guy named Keith in Cornwall on Hudson.
"How much is Sirius satellite radio or Howard Stern himself
paying the New York Post to keep the shock jock's name in the paper
on an almost daily basis? Get over him. He's old news."
Well, there you go. Evidently, I'm not old news.
Here's another one.
"Virtually every organization, both private and governmental,
has a code of conduct or a code of ethics that it expects its employees to obey.
If the vast majority of Stern's audience were to talk and act at their job
the way Stern and other Schlock jocks act, they'd be fired.
As Clint Eastwood might say to Stern, "A man's got to know his limitations."
Well, I'll respond to that. I don't have to know my limitations.
As a matter of fact, I came here because there are no limitations.
And quite frankly, your job is different than my job.
My job was constructed out of my own mind, out of my own boundaries, and breaking them.
So when you say you have to behave on your job, you're right. You do.
Because that's the profession you chose.
Not our problem.
Not my problem. I don't have to behave the way you behave at your job.
I get to break the rules.
I can look like a pig.
Yeah. I took the risks, so that's what I've earned.
You knew what you were doing.
The First Amendment states, here's another one.
"Congress shall make no law abridging the freedom of speech.
If the religious right takes this too far,
the Supreme Court will be forced to follow anything and everything on all media,
both free and for pay."
In the meantime, if you don't want to hear a fart,
head over to the channel and tune in to some decent radio,
such as the hate-spearing Pat Robertson.
And these go on and on and on.
But what was most interesting is that Chauncey Hayden, once again,
now wrote the New York Post and the letters to the editor,
and seems to be on some sort of campaign against me.
Here he goes. Please, enough with stern. I beg of you.
This guy is so jealous. You know, Beth said to me the other night,
"Boy, you have this guy pegged. He said he's crazy. He's insane."
I said, "That's why I told you not to write to him."
Yeah, I was going to say, you know,
she had a little correspondence by email with him for a while,
and I was very afraid for her.
But he says, "Enough of you? Why doesn't he shut up about you?"
He keeps taking gigs to talk about it.
Here is a guy I'll suggest to you who is so jealous of me,
has tried for years to be me, went on the Internet radio,
and thought he was going to have a successful radio program
just banging chicks or getting blowjobs.
Well, he thought that was taking you one step further, I guess.
He begged us for a job on our radio station, was a super, super fan,
and now that we've rejected him has taken the opposite approach
to get our attention by writing negative letters
and trying to go anywhere he can to bad-mouth me.
Listen to this letter. Please, enough with stern. I beg of you.
Jesus has come back for an encore in the form of a six-foot-seven scarecrow.
Well, this scarecrow beat you in a beauty pageant, you ugly fuck.
I remember that, yeah. It's a beauty pageant.
But who knew that Jesus would be worshipped the second time around
for describing a woman over 120 pounds as fat?
So now he has a problem with the way I treat women.
Well, let me tell you something.
This is a guy who treated women like dog shit on his Internet radio show.
This is a guy who worshipped at my feet until I cast him aside.
Isn't this the guy who tries to lure women into his basement
so he can take pictures of them?
Yeah, and he's trying to seduce girls with one arm because he has an amputee fetish.
Right.
I love this letter.
Or what about that he would pick the bones of the deceased show regular Debbie Tae
out of her wooden urn? Well, let me remind you.
Oh, my goodness.
He's the asswipe who brought the urn down to her radio station.
All we can do is ask if you had the ashes.
He brought her in, and he claimed he was her good friend.
Wow, wow, wow. She was your good friend because she had access to the show.
We didn't have her ashes. You brought them to us.
Who knew that our lord and savior, Stern, would be pouring booze
down the throat of a pathetic drunken dwarf while millions laughed in amusement?
Well, no. I never poured any booze down the throat of anyone.
Again, a libelous and slanderous statement.
And he's dangerously close to libel.
Oh, not dangerously close. He's lively.
Well, he's over the line.
That's right.
But here we are. I agree. Howard is charming.
Now, can we please wish the god of all media all the best and move on?
Well, it seems to me the only person who's keeping my name out there is you,
you fuck nut, because you are the one who is going to the New York Post.
You're the one going to Fox News, and the only attention you get is off my name.
Case closed.
And finally, this letter. I just like this.
Chauncey Hayden of Manhattan. Isn't he from Jersey?
Yeah.
Okay, that's what I thought.
The only thing that has kept stern in the spotlight and on all the -- sorry.
The only thing that has kept stern in the spotlight and on the air all these years
is his skill at stirring controversy around the issue of corporate and government censorship.
With the switch to satellite broadcasting, the issue becomes moot.
It'll be interesting to see if he continues to have any relevance to anyone
other than those who enjoy a good fart joke.
Well, Michael from Bayside, my career was never about the FCC,
particularly when I first started out.
I was the number one dish jockey in Washington, D.C., and there was no FCC hounding me,
as you say, that I need hounding from the government in order to have what you call as an act.
Well, here's the ironic thing. The government is hounding me more than ever.
They're talking about regulating satellite.
They're trying to figure out how to keep involved.
So if my strength is the government hounding me, well, guess what? It's still here.
You haven't lost your partner.
No, the government is still going to keep me popular.
But I'll suggest this. I've been on the radio many years without FCC interference.
And, boy, my ratings were never higher than when I didn't have FCC interference.
So you tell me, who do you bet on? Who's your money on?
So we'll see.
Anyway, so that's from the New York Post.
Again, every single letter to the editor is about me.
And I thought we would go off into oblivion in the world of satellite.
But they're just making the show more and more popular.
I've got to mention something.
As the king of all blacks said yesterday, didn't know you could get bigger.
Yeah, well, I have.
I think he said, I didn't know you could get bigger.
And by the way, word's going to leak out.
The more programming we put on and the more that's going on over here at Siri-ish, you'll see.
It's just going to keep going and going and going.
And for all those people who think satellite radio isn't a business, let's wait and find out.
Let's see.
Thank you, sir.
Thank you.
And Fred, that's going to be your role on Really Desperate Housewives.
You're never going to talk.
Even if they have an argument, it's going to be done with such effects.
What's up with that?
And she's just going to go berserk.
Shut your mouth.
And Mary Delabate is the best.
Turns out she's an anthropologist and she's just been observing him like--
Like Margaret Mead?
Like Margaret Mead studied ancient cultures.
Like Jane Goodall.
Yeah, like Jane Goodall.
That's what Sam said, like Jane Goodall.
He's not even an ancient culture.
Do you ever explain why she mates with her subject?
She's way into it.
She seems to study in you, learning your habits.
Well, she wondered what would happen if a human interacted with you.
Well, maybe she even made it with another guy and you don't know it.
Who knows where Sam goes with this evil, evil sitcom
that we're going to be here every year.
I want to know at one point during the Fred argument,
he pulls out the nigga please.
All I know is Tim called me yesterday so excited.
He says, Sam, I'm going to call you to talk about the sitcom.
And I said, what sitcom did Sam want to write?
And when he said Really, Really Desperate Housewives, I said, you're kidding.
I didn't think anyone would pick up on that.
And that's the one Sam was most attracted to.
Because I pitched him a couple of other ones.
He wants to develop for our network Really, Really Desperate Housewives.
You know what?
Sometimes just being the slob that I am, I sit down and I've become friends with him.
I try to figure out how much money he has.
He has points.
I believe he has points in Taxi, Cheers, The Tracy O'Lemond Show, The Simpsons and Drew Carrey.
Well, that's why it's so astounding to me that he will write our sitcoms.
I mean, you're that big of a fan of the show.
He's that big of a fan show.
And he told me he's been at his card game.
Here's a card game with some pretty high-powered--
I got in it once.
And believe me, it's not fun.
I actually sat on the slide.
So he was telling me that all the guys at his card game all have serious satellite radio now.
Right.
They've all gone out and made the purchase.
And he goes, it's really weird how it's happening.
He says, I see it.
And these are guys who are so jaded, don't care about shit.
And they've gone out.
And they have everything, right?
Yeah.
And they went out and they bought it.
And they talk more about it now than they did the old show.
Right.
Well, I'm telling you, if you start listening, and sometimes I make the mistake of turning
it on after our show, you get started with that after show.
Well--
It rolls right into the news.
You can't stop listening.
Well, you're absolutely right.
I listened to the after show for the first time.
I was listening.
I'm a big fan of Howard 101.
I don't know how many people spend more time.
Well, that's where Bubba is.
That's where Bubba is.
Right.
And I listened to-- I heard the very end of our show.
I wanted to hear what it sounded like on the replay because, as you know, on Howard 101,
it is set up for people on the West Coast to hear the show.
Right.
It was ending.
And then the-- what I call the after show, what Gary calls the wrap-up show--
Yeah, the after show is a much better name.
It is a terrific show.
I like after shit.
Yeah.
It is a great, great show.
Yesterday's wrap-up show, dare I say-- and I hate to admit this-- was better than our show.
Well, I'll tell you, the other day I did the show for the first time after the revelations.
Yeah.
I was like, boy, this is fun.
It's fun to do.
Did you happen to catch my parents last night?
No, I missed that part.
John Hein--
Had to stop.
John Hein is a great fan.
Shut down.
I'm so proud of this guy.
This is a guy who we met through the website Jump the Shark.
And this guy, you know, when Gary first said, well, maybe John should host the super fan roundtable,
you know, I kind of went, well, let's give it a shot.
But who knows what to expect?
This guy is such a kind.
He's so meant for a radio career.
He will be with me as long as I'm here.
And he's so happy to be here.
Yeah, he's great.
What was his job before?
What was he doing?
What was he doing to really make money?
Because he's got a family and everything.
He worked for Princeton Review, which is one of those places where you go to get tutored
when you have to take the SATs and, like, law stuff.
And I think he was like--
Wasn't he ahead of marketing?
I think he was ahead of marketing.
Yeah, something like that.
Brilliant guy, everything he touches.
He was so good with my parents last night.
The hour flew by.
I wish they had done four hours.
It was that good.
And they're never going to do another one?
John, well, I've got to talk to you about that.
I have this weird sort of feeling about--
Really?
--putting my parents out there.
Like, even afterwards, Ralph called up.
He goes, oh, when your father went-- he almost did this.
And he almost-- and he was just like, I don't know if I want everyone more.
Oh, you don't have to be stupid.
You're more--
Well, Ralph is particularly difficult.
Because I think he'd like them to be his parents, so he picks them apart.
Yeah, yeah.
He's got a whole thing going with my parents.
It's a little creepy.
Did you hear mom and dad?
I saw the notes from Jim as one of the producers who works on the show.
And he said that it's actually sort of cute, but your dad comes prepared with notes.
Does he?
Yeah, he's all prepared.
What are his notes?
I don't know.
I've got to go look at what he wrote.
That's good.
I can tell you.
He had about three pages on a legal pad of different things written out.
And during the first break, we were talking a little bit.
And I said, Mr. Stern, is there anything that we haven't covered yet?
